rulings.Case Title: PERFORMING ARTS LLC A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY VS. MICHAEL MURRAY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn For Order Requiring Certain X-Complainants To File An Undertaking In The Amount Of $106,822.27
rulings.Rulings: 1Matter on calendar on Monday, August 4, 2014, Line 1, DEFENDANTs LEO CASSIDY, JAMES NUNEMACHER, DEIRDRE CASSIDY, LJ SF INVESTMENTS LLC, 973 MARKET STREET, LLC and THE CHRIS JAMES CORPORATION DBA VANGUARD PROPERTIES' Motion For Order Requiring Certain Cross-Complainants To File An Undertaking In The Amount Of $106,822.27. The motion is denied. First, CCP 1030 does not apply to cross-complainants. See Yao v. Superior Court (2002) 104 Cal.App.4th 327, 332-333. Second, cross-defendants have absolutely failed to sustain their evidentiary burden. Cross-defendants present no evidence showing that they have a reasonable possibility of obtaining judgment in their favor. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JKS)

rulings.Case Title: ROY POOLE VS. CALIFORNIA PACIFIC MEDICAL FOUNDATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Roy Poole Notice Of Motion For Order Permitting Plaintiff To Augment Expert Disclosure
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 4, 2014, Line 2, Plaintiff Roy Poole Motion For Order Permitting Plaintiff To Augment Expert Disclosure. (Part 1 of 3). Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Poole's Motion to Augment is GRANTED in part and DENIED in part. Poole's Amended Disclosure of Experts is SUSTAINED with the following addendum: Dr. Leslie Zun's testimony shall be limited to opining to as to the standard of care for nurses in the emergency department setting. Poole shall make Dr. Zun available for deposition no later than fourteen days prior to trial. Poole's request for sanctions is DENIED. Although Poole is the prevailing party, CPMC (not Poole) shall prepare a form of the order consistent with this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. Poole shall approve the proposed order as to form or explain why he declines to so approve. In the absence of a hearing, the CPMC shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312 as if CPMC was the prevailing party. "On motion of any party who has engaged in a timely exchange of expert witness information, the court may grant leave to . . . [a]ugment that party's expert witness list and declaration by adding the name and address of any expert witness whom that party has subsequently retained." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2034.610. A motion to augment an expert witness list shall be granted only upon considering the "the extent to which the opposing party has relied on the list of expert witnesses" and upon a finding that the opposing party "will not be prejudiced in maintaining that party's action or defense on the merits." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2034.620(a)-(b). In addition, to grant the motion, the Court must also find one of the following two conditions to obtain: that (1) the "moving party" did not fail in the "exercise of reasonable diligence" to "call that expert witness . . . or have decided to offer the different or additional testimony of that expert witness"; or (2) the movant's failure to "call that expert witness, or to offer the different or additional testimony of that expert witness" was "a result of mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect, and the moving party has . . .[s]ought leave to augment . . . promptly after deciding to call the expert witness" and "thereafter served a copy of the proposed expert witness information concerning the expert . . . on all other parties." Id. § 2034.620(c). (Continued to Part 2). =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ROY POOLE VS. CALIFORNIA PACIFIC MEDICAL FOUNDATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Roy Poole'S Motion For Order Permitting Plaintiff To Augment Expert Disclosure (For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only - Part 2 Of 3)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 4, 2014, Line 2, Plaintiff Roy Poole'S Motion For Order Permitting Plaintiff To Augment Expert Disclosure (For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only - Part 2 Of 3). These facts present a close issue under a section 2034.620. On the one hand Plaintiff Roy Poole ("Poole") acted appropriately in withdrawing his RN expert Nurse Dennis Bietz and replacing him with Dr. Leslie Zun after determining that Nurse Bietz medical injury would make him unavailable for trial. On the other hand, Defendant Sutter West Bay Hospitals dba California Pacific Medical Center ("CPMC") has certainly relied upon Poole's disclosure of Nurse Bietz in formulating its discovery plan and trial strategy, and now with only weeks to go on a continued trial date, would certainly be prejudiced by the replacement of a nurse expert for a doctor expert. And while some cases suggest it would be an abuse of discretion for this Court to deny Poole's request to substitute an expert witness when the earlier-named expert becomes unavailable through illness, it is far from clear that Poole acted diligently and promptly, in augmenting the expert witness list and then bringing the instant Motion. On the question of diligence, CPMC argues it was not served the Augmented Disclosure until it stumbled upon the issue in a mid-July 2014 e-mail meet and confer. Poole argues that the issue was discussed earlier, but the evidence of this is scant. More troubling is that even if we assume that the Augmented Disclosure was served on July 7, 2014 as stated in Poole's proof of service, it is not entirely clear why the service was made via mail instead of personally or why, as the clock ticked down toward trial, the instant Motion was not filed for many weeks after that (on July 23, 2014). Poole argues that he warned of this contingency, but in fact, his original Expert Disclosure noted Ronald Morrell's anticipated withdrawal due to back injury, not Nurse Bietz. Poole argues he should not be penalized for this harmless error. But the error is clearly not harmless to CPMC who relied upon this disclosure in developing its trial strategy. Perhaps the crux of the issue is whether CPMC would be prejudiced by the fact that Poole seeks to replace Nurse Bietz with Dr. Leslie Zun. On this both parties agree: Dickison stands for the proposition that no party suffers prejudice by augmentation where the new expert opines as to the exact same set of issues as the earlier named expert. See Dickison v. Howen, 220 Cal. App. 3d 1471, 1479 (1990). But here Poole wants to replace a nurse with a doctor. And although both professionals-Nurse Bietz and Dr. Zun-have been noticed by Poole as opining as to the "standard of care for emergency room medicine" in fact Nurse Bietz qualifications necessarily limit his opinions to the edge of his professional knowledge and experience-and no more. As Nurse Bietz explained in his declaration in Opposition to Summary Judgment "I am familiar with the standard of care and standard operating procedures for nurses in the emergency department setting." Notably Nurse Bietz did not claim to have an understanding as to the standard of care for doctors. As Dr. Zun's qualifications are more expansive than Nurse Bietz, and in some sense perhaps more authoritative, his opinions will not be the "same" as Nurse Bietz. Thus, without a limiting instruction of some sort, CPMC will certainly suffer prejudice as a consequence of Poole's expert substitution. For these reasons, and in order to mitigate the prejudice as against CPMC, see § 2034.620(b), the Court will permit augmentation but circumscribe the conditions on "other terms as may be just," § 2034.620(d). (Continued to Part 3.) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTINE FONG et al VS. DIGNITY HEALTH, INC. et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Monday, August 4, 2014, Line 13, DEFENDANT DIGNITY HEALTH DBA ST MARY'S MEDICAL CENTER's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Sustained. (CCP sec. 430.10(e).) The demurrer to the second cause of action for elder abuse is sustained with 10 days leave to amend. Plaintiffs are given leave to amend to allege specific facts that Defendant acted recklessly and an officer, director, or managing agent engaged, authorized, or ratified the conduct. (Civ. Code sec. 3294(b).) The demurrer to the first cause of action for willful misconduct is sustained without leave to amend. "[T]he need for a separate category of negligence identified as willful misconduct, which was designed to alleviate the inequity of the all-or-nothing contributory negligence rule, has disappeared with the adoption of a rule of comparative negligence." (Southern Pacific Transportation Co. v. The State of California (1981) 115 Cal.App.3d 116, 121.) The demurrer to the fourth cause of action for negligence per se is sustained without leave to amend. Negligence per se is an evidentiary doctrine, not a cause of action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number.
